concerns.text: User Story 2 tasks (T020-T024) depend on binary outputs from User Story 1 (T011-T017), but the task list places US2 implementation tasks *after* US1 implementation tasks without explicit dependency markers or ordering constraints. While the 'Dependencies & Execution Order' section mentions this, the task list itself must enforce that US2 tasks cannot be started until US1 binaries are generated. The current flat list implies US2 could be attempted before US1 completes, violating the producer-consumer flow.

User Story 3 tasks (T027-T032) require 'block-averaged latency and error data' from US1 and US2. T027 (block-averaging) and T028 (t-test) depend on the aggregated CSVs from T023 (US2) and T015/T016 (US1). The task list does not explicitly sequence T027/T028 to occur *after* T023 and T015/T016 completion. Without explicit ordering, a developer might attempt statistical analysis before the necessary data artifacts (stability_metrics.csv, raw timing logs) are produced.

T015 (executor) and T016 (memory fallback) produce the raw latency logs and binary outputs. T017 (NaN detection) consumes these outputs. However, T017 is listed as a separate task *after* T015/T016. If T017 is implemented as a post-processing step, it must explicitly depend on T015/T016 completion. The current list does not clarify if T017 is part of the executor logic (T015) or a separate analysis step, creating ambiguity in the data-flow dependency.

Test tasks T009 and T010 are marked [P] (parallel-safe) and placed before implementation tasks T011-T017. While the 'Implementation Strategy' section states 'Tests MUST be written and FAIL before implementation', the task list itself does not enforce this ordering. The [P] tag implies they can run in parallel with other tasks, but they logically depend on the *existence* of the code they test (T011-T017) to fail. This creates a semantic contradiction: tests cannot be written/failed before the code exists, yet the list suggests they can be done in parallel or before.
